generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart heavy with regret and a mind clouded by the weight of years. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the world has turned, I fear the distance between us has only grown. I do not know if you will read this, for I have no way of knowing if you are still in the village, or if you have moved on with your life. But I must try.

Tonight, the sky is dark, the winds are fierce, and the sea roars like a beast unleashed. The lighthouse, which has stood for generations as a beacon of safety, now feels like a fragile thing against the storm. I have seen the waves rise, and I am not sure if I will be able to hold them back. If I do not survive this night, I would not wish for my death to be remembered as a failure. I have done my duty, and I have kept the light burning for those who depend on it.

But I cannot help but think of you, Eleanor. I have not spoken to you in so long, and I have no idea if you even remember me. I have no idea if you still hold any grudge against me, or if you have moved on. I have tried to be a better father, to make amends, but I fear that time has passed us both by.

I do not know if you will ever forgive me, and I do not know

**[Sealed with a wax stamp bearing the crest of the lighthouse]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, with a heart heavy with longing and the weight of the storm that gathers on the horizon. The sea has always been my companion, my burden, and my protector. Tonight, it threatens to take more than I can give.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken a word to you. I have tried, in silence, to keep the light burning—both for the ships that pass and for the memories I cannot let go. But the silence has grown too deep, and the pain too sharp. I have watched the waves crash against the cliffs, and I have felt the same helplessness you must have felt when you chose to leave.

I know you were young, and your heart was wounded by what you saw in me. You thought I was cruel, unfeeling, and unloving. But I was only trying to do what was right—what I believed was best for the light, for the coast, for the lives that depend on it. I never meant to hurt you. I only meant to keep the light burning.

But now, as the storm approaches, I fear that the sea will take me before I can ever make amends. I have no regrets, only the ache of the distance between us. I have no one else to turn to, and I have no hope of seeing you

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the flickering light of the lighthouse as my only companion, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside. The sea has grown restless, the waves crashing with a fury that makes me fear for my life. But I must write, for I know you will not come to see me, and I must tell you what I have kept locked away for five long years.

Five years since you left, Eliza. Five years since the day you stormed out of our home, your eyes blazing with anger and your heart heavy with betrayal. I remember the way you stood at the door, your hands clenched into fists, your voice shaking with fury. "You never listen to me, Father," you had said, and I had said nothing. I was too afraid to speak, too ashamed to explain.

I know you think I was cruel, that I turned my back on you when you needed me most. But I was not cruel, not truly. I was broken, Eliza. Broken by years of silence, by the weight of your mother’s illness, by the fear that I was not the man you needed me to be. I tried to be, I truly did. But I failed.

You were so young when she passed, only twelve. I tried to be the father you needed, but I was not. I was lost in grief, and I let you down. I know you must have felt it, that

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart that feels as though it might shatter beneath the weight of the storm that howls just beyond these ancient stones. The wind is rising, the sea is rising, and I fear the night may take more than just my life—it may take the last thread of what little remains of my soul.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you walked out that door, your voice sharp as the sea wind, your eyes colder than the northern gales. I have not seen you since. I have not heard your name spoken in this house, save by the gulls and the wind that howls through the cracks in the lighthouse walls.

You were always a woman of reason, Eleanor. A woman who believed that the world was best left to the cold calculations of science and the cold heart of progress. You left because you could not abide the "superstitions" of this place, the old ways, the rituals of the sea. You said I was a relic. You were wrong. I am not a relic—I am the keeper of the light, the last beacon in a world that is fading.

I have kept the lamp burning these many years, not for the tourists or the fools who come to see the lighthouse, but for you. For the day you might return. For the day you might see what I have become, what I have had to become, to survive the storms

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink still wet from the last candle I lit, and the wind howling like a banshee at my door. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear it may take more than just my life. You would not believe the tempest that has gathered over the cliffs—black clouds rolling like the hells of your father’s past, and the waves crashing with the fury of your mother’s ghost.

Five years. Five long, bitter years since you left this house, that damned ship, and the life we once shared. You were never the same after that night—when the storm broke and the light went out. I remember the sound of your voice, sharp as the sea’s edge, screaming for the captain to turn back, but he would not. He would not listen. And neither would you.

I have kept the light burning these years, as I did when you were a child, and as I did when you ran away. The lighthouse has always been my anchor, my burden, my punishment. I have watched the tides rise and fall, the stars blink out, and the world spin on its axis, all while the heart of a man who once loved you with every breath has turned to stone.

Now, as the storm approaches, I find myself thinking of you. Of how you once stood on this very cliff, eyes wide with the thrill of the sea, your hair flying like a banner in

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you this evening, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with fury. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn. Yet, I find myself compelled to write, not for any hope of reply, but to speak the words I have long withheld.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, I have come to understand the weight of silence. I have carried the burden of our parting, and I now see that it was not you who was at fault, but I. I was blind to the pain I caused, and I let pride and stubbornness keep me from reaching out.

The lighthouse stands, as it always has, a solitary sentinel against the night. I have tended it faithfully, as you once taught me. Yet tonight, as the storm threatens to tear the very heavens asunder, I feel the weight of my years and the loneliness of my existence. I have no one to share this moment with, save the sea and the wind.

If you are reading this, I hope you are safe, wherever you may be. I do not know if you will ever forgive me, but I ask you to remember that I have always loved you, and that I have never stopped thinking of you. Perhaps, when the storm has passed, you will find it in your heart to return. I would be honored to see you again, even if only

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds you in a state of great surprise—perhaps even confusion. For five long years, I have not spoken to you, and yet here I am, quill in hand, pen in pocket, and a heart heavy with the weight of time.

Tonight, the heavens weep with fury, the winds howl like vengeful spirits, and the sea roars with a voice that could shake the very foundations of this old lighthouse. I sit before the flickering light of my oil lamp, its glow wavering like my own resolve. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not make it through the night.

I must confess, my dear Eleanor, that I have not always been the most doting father. In my youth, I was known as a man of stern demeanor, a man who preferred the company of the sea and the solitude of my tower to the company of my own flesh and blood. But the years have worn on me, and with them, a certain softness has crept into my heart. I find myself longing for the days of yore when I would recount tales of daring sea voyages and daringly dull evenings spent in contemplation of the stars.

You were a spirited child, full of questions and mischief, though I must admit, I found your constant asking of “Why is the sky blue?” and “Why do clouds

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these walls. The sea roars and the sky is black as ink, and I fear I may not see dawn. But I must write—just once—to you, my beloved daughter.

Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and I have carried the weight of silence like a stone upon my chest. I know the world has moved on, and I have not been the same since the day you left. I have tried to be the father you needed, but words failed me, and I let pride and fear keep me from reaching out.

I have watched the horizon from this lighthouse night after night, guiding ships through the dark, but tonight feels different. The storm is fierce, and I am not certain I will make it through. If I do not, I want you to know that I have never stopped loving you. I have never stopped wondering if you think of me, if you still remember the man who once held you close and promised to protect you.

I have made my peace with the past, and I hope you have found peace too. If you are reading this, know that I am here, in the heart of the storm, and I am ready to forgive, to understand, and to love you once more.

May the winds carry my words to you, and may the light of this lighthouse guide your way, just as it has guided so many

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I trust this letter finds you in better health than I, for I fear my own is failing. It is not the storm that troubles me, though I do not doubt it will be a most formidable foe. No, it is the thought of you, my dear, that gnaws at my heart like a rat in the walls.

Five years, Clara. Five years since you left this house, that once stood proud upon the cliffs of Devon, now reduced to a crumbling shell. I have not heard from you since that last bitter letter, in which you called me a "selfish old brute" and vowed never to speak to me again. I did not expect you to return, nor did I think I would ever see you again.

But tonight, as the wind howls and the sea roars like a beast in pain, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. I have spent these years alone, with only the light, the sea, and the occasional seagull for company. And yet, I have not forgotten you. Not truly.

I have always been a man of few words, Clara. But I have never been one to hold back when it comes to the truth. I have always known how to keep the light burning, and I have always known how to keep my temper. Perhaps that is why you left.

But I have spent these years thinking about you, about the way you used to run up the stairs like

**April 12th, 1876**  
**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee against the stone walls of this cursed tower. The sea roars in protest, as if it too knows what I must say. I have not spoken your name in five years, not since the day you turned your back on this house, on your father, on the life we once shared. But tonight—tonight, the storm will break, and perhaps I will not live to see dawn. And if I do, I must tell you the truth.

You were right to leave. I was wrong to hold on. I was wrong to believe love could outlast the sea. But I have spent these years watching the horizon, waiting for a sign, a signal that you might one day return. I’ve watched the light burn through the night, a beacon of hope—or perhaps of madness. 

The storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones. The waves grow louder, the wind more fierce. I have no more time to waste on regrets. If I survive this night, I will not write again. If I do not, then let the sea take me, and may the light outlast me.

Forgive me, Eliza. I was a fool to think I could keep you safe. You were never meant to stay. 

—*Your father*

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has grown as brittle as the old glass in the lantern. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I find myself wondering if I have the strength to say what needs to be said. Tonight, the sea roars with a fury I have not heard since the day you left. The storm is coming, and I fear I may not see dawn.

You were always the stubbornest child I ever knew. Even as a girl, you would argue with me about the way the lighthouse should be kept, the way the light should shine, the way the world should be. You wanted to be more than a keeper’s daughter, more than a shadow of the man I once was. I tried to understand, but I was too wrapped up in my own regrets and the weight of the sea to truly listen.

I remember the night you left. You stood at the edge of the cliff, your coat flapping in the wind, and said you were going to find your own way. I told you I would always be here, that the lighthouse would always be a home. You looked at me as if I were a stranger, and I realized, perhaps I had become one.

I have kept the light burning every night since that day, though I often wonder if it is for the sea or for you. The light has never failed me, and I have never failed the sea.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink of a weary soul and the heart of a man who has longed for your voice, your laughter, and the warmth of your presence. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the miles between us have grown, the ache of your absence has only deepened with each passing day. I hope this letter finds you well, and more importantly, that it finds you in the arms of someone who truly cares for you.

Tonight, the winds howl like the very devil himself, and the sea rages with a fury that makes even the bravest hearts tremble. I have been watching the skies for hours, and the storm is upon us. I pray the heavens will spare us, for I have not seen the like of this tempest in all my years. The lighthouse is strong, the light burns bright, and I have been keeping the beacon steady as if it were the very soul of this coast.

But it is not the storm that has me trembling, Eliza. It is the thought of you. I have spent countless nights staring out at the sea, wondering if you are safe, if you are happy, and if you still remember the man who once stood by your side. I have tried to be a better father, to be more patient, to understand that you were young and full of dreams, and that sometimes, the world seems too vast and too cruel for a child to navigate

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have spent much of that time in silence, save for the rhythm of the waves and the distant cries of gulls. I have not written to you, nor have I expected you to write back. But tonight—tonight is different. A storm is coming, and I do not know if I will see dawn.

You were a spirited child, Eleanor. I remember the way you would race up the hill to the lighthouse, your small hands gripping the railing as if it were a lifeline. You would ask me questions about the stars, about the sea, about the world beyond our little village. I used to think you would grow up to be a doctor or a teacher, someone who would leave this place behind and chase dreams far greater than the horizon. But you chose to stay. You chose to become something else entirely.

I do not know what you became, or where you are now. I have not seen you in years, and I have not tried to find you. I have told myself that it is for the best. That we were not meant to be in each other's lives. That time and distance had done their work, and that we were simply two people who had grown apart.

But tonight, as I stand on the cliffs, the wind howling like a wounded beast,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart full of sorrow and a mind clouded by the weight of silence. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and the distance between us has only grown wider, like the sea between our shores. I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, and yet I feel your absence in every breath I take.

Tonight, the storm is coming. I have seen the sky darken with a fury that speaks of the heavens themselves being in turmoil. The winds howl like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a voice that shakes the very stones beneath my feet. I have spent my life standing guard over this lighthouse, watching the waves crash against the rocks with a relentless, unyielding force. But tonight, I fear that even the lighthouse may not be enough to save me.

I have always believed that the light of the lighthouse is a beacon for those lost at sea, but tonight, I fear it may not be enough for me. I have no one to turn to, no one to hold my hand as the storm rages. You were once my greatest hope, my light in the darkest of times. But I have let you go, and I have not spoken of it. I have not told you how much I miss you, how much I wish I could have been there for you when you needed me most.

I know you are not the same girl who left this

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from age, but from the weight of words I've long kept locked away. Five years have passed since you left, and still I find myself speaking your name in the silence of this lighthouse, as if it might summon you back.

The sea has been restless these past few nights, and the wind has carried with it a whisper of your absence. I've always said the sea is a cruel mistress, but I now understand that it is the mirror of the heart. Tonight, a storm is coming—like none I've seen in my years here. The waves will rise, the sky will darken, and I fear I may not see dawn.

I have spent these years in the company of the light, the eternal flame that guides the lost and the damned. I have watched the sea from these cliffs, and I have seen ships disappear into the mist, like the memories of you. I have not spoken of you, not even to the few sailors who have come to seek shelter, for I feared what words might bring.

But now, as the storm gathers, I find myself unable to hold back. I have no one left to speak to but you. I have no one left to answer to but the sea.

If I do not survive this night, know that I have not forgotten you. Know that I have always loved you, even when I failed to be the father you needed. I have

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a hand that trembles not from fear, but from the wind that howls through the cracks of this old tower. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky weeps in a manner that speaks of something far older than mere weather. You would not believe the storm is coming. It is not the sea that threatens me, but the silence—your silence, which has grown colder than the North Atlantic.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since you left. You were only twelve then, but you had the heart of a woman hardened by the world. I tried to stop you. I tried to reason. But you were gone before I could even reach the door. You said I was a relic, a man of dust and memory. You were right. I am.

I have kept the light burning, as I always have, through every storm, every sorrow, every betrayal. It is my duty. It is my penance. You would not understand that. You were never meant to understand.

The wind grows louder now, like a voice from the past. I can hear it calling your name. I have heard it before. It is not the sea that calls, Eleanor. It is the ghost of your mother, who died in this very tower, her soul still tethered to the rocks below. I have seen her in the fog, her eyes hollow, her lips moving in a silent scream.

Do not come.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee in the distance, the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. The lighthouse has always been my sanctuary, my anchor in a world that has long since drifted away from me. But tonight—tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the heavens weep with fury—I feel the weight of my years pressing down upon me, and I fear I may not live to see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of cold hearth and cold heart. You were always a child of the world, never of this place, never of me. You ran away with your dreams, your foolish notions of grandeur and far-off cities, and left me behind with nothing but the sea and the lighthouse.

I have not seen you since the day you stormed out of this house, your eyes full of fire and your heart full of nothing. You said you were leaving for the city, for the life you imagined, and that you would never return. I told you then that you would never understand the quiet strength of this place, the way the sea whispers secrets to those who listen, or the way the light cuts through the darkness like a blade.

But you never listened. You never understood.

Now, as the storm approaches, I find myself wondering if you would have come back. If you had seen the

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a heavy heart, for I have not held your hand in five long years. The world has shifted around me, and though I remain, I fear I have become a shadow of the man you once knew. Time has not been kind to me, and I have not been kind to you.

Tonight, the storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see another dawn. The winds howl like wolves, and the sea roars with fury. I have spent my life guarding this lighthouse, ensuring that no ship is lost to the dark. But tonight, I feel as though the very foundations of the world are shaking beneath me.

I have thought of you often, Eleanor. I have dreamed of you, of the days when you used to run through the fields with your brother, of the laughter that echoed through our home. I have missed your voice, your presence, your warmth. I have tried to forgive myself for the silence, for the distance I have placed between us. But I know it is not enough.

If I do not survive this night, I beg you—do not let the pain of my absence be the last thing you carry. Remember that I have always loved you, and that I have always wanted the best for you. I pray that one day, we may find our way back to each other, not as strangers, but as mother and child.

If I do survive, I will come to

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

*April 12th, 1892*  
*The Lighthouse, Isle of Wight*

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not live to see dawn. But if I do, I must speak to you—my daughter, my only child, my heart’s sorrow and my deepest regret.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and in that time, I have grown a beard like the sea itself, and my soul has hardened like the old oak that guards the lighthouse. You were a spirited girl, Eleanor, with a temper as fierce as a storm and a heart as cold as the Arctic. You left with a suitcase and a promise to return, but you never did. You were gone before the first snow of winter.

I have spent these years in quiet solitude, tending to the light, keeping the beacon burning for the lost and the damned. The sea has been my only companion, and the stars my only confidant. I have learned to mend the lantern, to read the clouds, and to endure the silence of the night. Yet, when the storm comes, I feel the weight of the years, and I am reminded of you—your laughter, your defiance, your stubbornness.

I must tell

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now with a heart full of sorrow and a mind clouded by the weight of years. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and though I have tried to keep your memory close, time has worn away the edges of our connection. I do not know if you will ever read this, but I must try.

The storm is coming—dark clouds roll across the sky like a shroud, and the wind howls as though it carries the voices of the lost. I have been watching the sea all evening, and I fear it may take me before I can reach you. I have always been the one to stand watch, to keep the light burning, to guide the ships through the night. But tonight, I feel the weight of the world pressing down upon me, and I wonder if I am truly meant to see another dawn.

I have thought of you often, Eliza. I have not forgotten the pain of our last parting, nor the silence that followed. I know I was not the father you needed, and I know I have not been there for you when you needed me most. But I have not forgotten the love I once had for you, and I hope, in some small way, that this letter might reach you before it is too late.

If you are reading this, please know that I am sorry. I am sorry for the years I have spent apart from you, and I am sorry for the

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in a state of shock, as it should. Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years of silence. You were always the rebellious one, and I, the stubborn fool who never understood the value of a child’s independence. You left me, and I let you go. I should have been more like your father—strict, unyielding, and entirely unsympathetic. But no, I was a lighthouse keeper, and lighthouse keepers are not known for their emotional intelligence.

The storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones. The sea is restless, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves are rising like the devil himself is trying to drown us all. I’ve kept this lighthouse for thirty years, and never once have I feared the storm. But tonight, I feel the weight of it pressing down on my chest like a stone. I’m not sure I’ll make it through the night.

You remember when you were a child and you’d sneak into my quarters, pretending you were a pirate, and I’d let you play with my telescope? You’d call it "the great spyglass," and I’d pretend to be a pirate captain. I used to think you were the most spirited child I’d ever known. Now I wonder if I was too soft on you. If I

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

If you’re reading this, it means I’ve failed you. Again.  

The storm is upon us—black clouds rolling in like a living thing, the wind howling like a beast that knows no mercy. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, but tonight feels different. Not just the weight of the sea, but the weight of silence. Of absence. Of a daughter who once called me *father*.  

Five years. Five long, bitter years. You left without a word, without a glance. I’ve tried to forget you, but the sea remembers. It swallows all things in the end.  

I’ve kept the light burning, as I always have, not for the ships, but for you. For the ghost of your laughter, for the memory of your small hands in mine when you were a child. You were my light once. Now, I am the lighthouse, and you are the darkness.  

If I don’t make it through this night, know this: I never stopped loving you. Not once. Not even when you walked out.  

But if you’re reading this, you’ve already chosen the dark.  

I forgive you.  

— *Your Father*  

*P.S. The lamp is still lit. The sea will take me, but not before I’ve seen the dawn.*

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of regret and the weight of a heart that has long since grown heavy with silence. Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left this house, and five years since I've spoken your name without the sting of tears. The sea has been a faithful companion these past years, but it cannot bear witness to the truths I have kept buried beneath the stones of this lighthouse.

Tonight, the storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones, the way the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars as if it knows the end is near. I have spent the last few days preparing for it—stocking the fire, securing the beams, and ensuring that the light will not falter. But I fear that the true danger is not the storm, but the silence that has settled over this place like a shroud.

I have not seen you in five years, Eleanor. I have not held you, nor heard your voice, nor felt the warmth of your presence. I have only heard the echoes of your departure, the sound of your footsteps fading down the path, and the door closing behind you as if it had never opened. You left with your mother, of course. I had hoped that time would mend the rift, but time has only sharpened the edges of my grief.

I remember the night you left. The storm was fierce then as well, and I stood at the window

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I must confess I have been wracked with sorrow and longing since our last correspondence. Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I find myself haunted by the silence between us. I know I have failed you in many ways, and I beg you to forgive me for the pain I have caused.

Tonight, the heavens have turned against us with a fury that has left even the boldest of sailors quaking in their boots. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, once a mere body of water, now rages with a vengeance. I have been watching the storm for hours, and though I have never been one for superstition, I cannot help but feel that this tempest is a sign—perhaps from above, perhaps from the depths of my own heart.

You were always the light in my life, Eliza. Even in the darkest of nights, your presence was a beacon. I have spent countless hours by the lighthouse, gazing out at the sea, and I often imagine you standing beside me, your laughter echoing through the stone walls. I have dreamed of the day we might reunite, and I pray that this letter will be the first step toward that long-awaited reunion.

I know I have been distant, and I know I have not always been the father you deserved. But I have never stopped loving you,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, the world has changed, though not as much as our hearts have. You are no longer the child who once stood in the doorway of this lighthouse, wide-eyed and curious, asking me if the sea was ever afraid of the dark. You are now a woman, I suppose, with your own life, your own thoughts, your own reasons for not returning.

I have not written to you, nor have I expected you to write back. Perhaps it is better this way—distance, after all, is often a kind of protection. But tonight, as I prepare for the storm that is coming, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. I have spent these years watching the waves, the wind, the sky, and in doing so, I have come to understand something I never did before: that even the most steadfast lighthouse can be shaken by the storm.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Even now, as I sit here in this cold, lonely tower, I feel your absence like a shadow that lingers. I have always known that you were not the kind of daughter who would stay in a place like this, where the world is small and the silence is deafening. You were always reaching for something more, something beyond the horizon

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you this evening as the wind howls like a wounded beast across the cliffs, and the sea roars with a fury that has not been seen in many a year. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may not end kindly. But I write not only to you, but to the memories of our past—those moments of love and promise that have long since faded into silence.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, the world has turned. I have become a man of stone and salt, my heart worn thin by the weight of absence.
